title: RubyGems.org API keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: RubyGems.org API key detected (32-character hexadecimal format). RubyGems.org API keys authenticate gem publishing and management operations. Exposing these keys allows attackers to publish malicious gem versions under your account, hijack gem ownership, yank legitimate gem versions causing supply chain disruptions, modify gem metadata and URLs to redirect users to malicious sites, inject backdoors into trusted gems affecting thousands of downstream Ruby applications, delete gems from the repository, and compromise the Ruby package ecosystem. Leaked API keys enable complete control over your RubyGems.org account including all published gems. Revoke this key immediately at https://rubygems.org/profile/edit, generate a new API key, rotate any related credentials, audit recent gem publishing activity for unauthorized changes, implement environment-based credential storage using ~/.gem/credentials with restricted file permissions, use separate API keys per environment, and monitor gem download statistics for anomalies indicating compromised packages.

RubyGems.org API key detected in gem push/yank command. The 'gem push' or 'gem yank' command with -k/--key parameter contains hardcoded gem publishing credentials. Exposing these keys allows unauthorized gem publishing, gem hijacking, malicious version injection, gem yanking causing dependency failures, and supply chain attacks on Ruby applications. Attackers can publish backdoored versions of your gems, inject malware into the Ruby ecosystem, yank critical gem versions disrupting thousands of projects, transfer gem ownership to malicious accounts, and compromise downstream applications trusting your gems. Never hardcode gem API keys in scripts or CI/CD configurations. Store API keys securely in ~/.gem/credentials with 0600 permissions, use environment variables ($RUBYGEMS_API_KEY), implement separate keys per environment, leverage secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Secrets), and monitor RubyGems.org activity logs for unauthorized gem operations.

RubyGems.org API key detected in variable assignment. RubyGems API keys provide authentication for publishing and managing Ruby gems. Exposing these keys in source code enables attackers to publish malicious gem versions, yank legitimate releases, hijack gem ownership, modify gem metadata, inject backdoors into trusted packages, and compromise the entire Ruby supply chain. Hardcoded API keys in repositories allow unauthorized access to your RubyGems.org account including all published gems. Revoke this key immediately from https://rubygems.org/profile/edit, generate a new API key with appropriate scopes, use ~/.gem/credentials file with strict permissions (chmod 0600), store keys in environment variables, implement secret management solutions (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GitHub Secrets), use separate API keys for different environments and CI/CD pipelines, enable two-factor authentication on RubyGems.org, monitor gem activity logs for suspicious publishing or yanking operations, and regularly rotate API keys every 90 days.
